meta.title: Southeast Asia Displacement Governance Mapping
meta.description: Framework-derived qualitative governance database: 8 countries, 7 governance areas, 3 fault lines, and chapter-linked case examples.
meta.method: Qualitative governance assessment derived from the accompanying chapter, policy briefs, secondary sources, and practitioner analysis. Not a ranking, scorecard, or primary data collection.

dimensions.name: Mobility governance
dimensions.desc: How migration, refugee, asylum, statelessness, and cross-border movement are recognised and governed.

dimensions.name: Disaster & internal displacement
dimensions.desc: How disaster, conflict, and internal displacement are governed across emergency and recovery phases.

dimensions.name: Public systems integration
dimensions.desc: How health, education, registration, labour, and social protection systems include or exclude displaced populations.

dimensions.name: Institutional responsibility
dimensions.desc: How mandates and coordination are allocated across ministries, local authorities, and external actors.

dimensions.name: Fiscal architecture
dimensions.desc: How displacement-related obligations are financed, transferred, bounded, or left outside ordinary budgets.

dimensions.name: Data & administrative infrastructure
dimensions.desc: How registries, identity systems, databases, and eligibility architecture follow — or fail to follow — people.

dimensions.name: Infrastructure & development finance
dimensions.desc: How safeguards, project finance, land acquisition, and development corridors shape displacement risk and absorption.

countries.name: Thailand
countries.role: Strongest absorption case: Health Insurance Card Scheme; pink cards/statelessness; Karen return reset.
countries.summary: Thailand combines restrictive refugee law with comparatively strong sectoral absorption in health. Its governance pattern shows how inclusion can advance through administrative and public-health incentives while remaining categorically separate from citizenship or asylum status.

mobility.characterization: Employer-tied labour migration and non-signatory refugee governance operate through reversible status, bounded documentation, and administrative discretion. UNHCR recognition and the National Screening Mechanism do not by themselves create pathways into ordinary systems.

disaster_idp.characterization: Disaster governance is comparatively institutionalized through the Ministry of Interior, but internal displacement is not governed through a distinct longitudinal framework. Stateless and border populations are managed through registration instruments that can restrict movement and reset access across provinces.

public_systems.characterization: Health is the strongest public-system absorption point. HICS extends coverage to non-Thai populations through a separate, premium-based and cost-recovery model, while most social assistance remains citizen-bound.
mechanisms: Health Insurance Card Scheme for Non-Thai Persons

institutional.characterization: No single whole-of-government displacement mandate exists. Coordination occurs through sectoral channels, cabinet discretion, and screening procedures rather than a unified architecture for displacement-related vulnerability.

fiscal.characterization: The HICS model limits open-ended fiscal exposure by separating non-citizen coverage from the citizen universal coverage scheme and using premium/cost-recovery logic. This makes absorption politically and fiscally manageable while preserving categorical boundaries.
mechanisms: Cost recovery through public health facilities

data.characterization: Administrative registration is relatively strong but fragmented. Disaster, health, welfare, migration, and stateless-person records do not form a single portable system following displaced populations across territorial or status changes.

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure-induced displacement is less central than in Mekong dam states, but Thailand is affected by cross-border downstream impacts and return dynamics along the Myanmar border. Development finance is not yet systematically connected to displacement absorption.

countries.name: Malaysia
countries.role: Categorical rigidity case: labour migration model extended to refugees; absorption ceiling shaped by citizenship and bumiputera politics.
countries.summary: Malaysia is the clearest categorical rigidity case. Refugees are managed through immigration law rather than protection architecture, and even limited functional access preserves a sharp separation from legal or fiscal incorporation.

mobility.characterization: Migration governance is built around employer-tied, temporary, reversible labour migration. Refugees without lawful immigration status remain treated as irregular migrants despite UNHCR documentation.

disaster_idp.characterization: Disaster management is organized around national disaster response but does not systematically include non-citizen or undocumented populations. Internal displacement is not a significant recognized policy category.

public_systems.characterization: Social protection programmes are overwhelmingly citizen or lawful-worker based. Refugees can access some health services at foreign-patient rates but are excluded from ordinary assistance, social insurance, and formal labour access.

institutional.characterization: The Ministry of Home Affairs and Immigration dominate. There is no national displacement framework or cross-ministerial architecture capable of separating functional access from membership incorporation.

fiscal.characterization: Fiscal architecture reinforces exclusion by reserving social assistance and insurance systems for citizens or documented workers. Refugee inclusion is framed as potential fiscal and membership exposure rather than resilience investment.

data.characterization: UNHCR registration functions as de facto identification but has no domestic legal effect. Refugee, immigration, health, and education data remain separated, preventing system-linked inclusion.

infra_finance.characterization: Development-induced displacement is not the central mapped governance problem, though Orang Asli and land-related displacement raise protection questions outside refugee and migration systems.

countries.name: Indonesia
countries.role: Palu, e-KTP/SIKS-NG, Presidential Regulation 125/2016, and fiscal-jurisdictional reset.
countries.summary: Indonesia has some of the region’s strongest formal architecture and data systems, but fiscal decentralisation and territorial anchoring limit continuity when people move across districts or emergency phases.

mobility.characterization: Indonesia is primarily a labour-sending state but has a formal refugee-handling instrument. Presidential Regulation 125/2016 assigns state responsibilities but does not create integration pathways or resolve local fiscal implementation gaps.

disaster_idp.characterization: BNPB provides strong emergency coordination, but support is time-bound. When emergency status expires, responsibility shifts to local government budgets, creating gaps for households whose displacement continues.

public_systems.characterization: Indonesia has significant social protection and health-system capacity through PKH, SIKS-NG, and BPJS Kesehatan, including adaptive enrolment in crises. The problem is not system absence but portability and eligibility continuity.

institutional.characterization: Formal whole-of-government design exists for refugees and disasters, but implementation is fragmented through decentralised fiscal and administrative authority across hundreds of districts.

fiscal.characterization: The fiscal constraint is jurisdictional. Emergency budgets can activate rapidly, but longer-term responsibility must be absorbed by local governments whose budget ceilings do not automatically follow displaced populations.

data.characterization: The e-KTP and SIKS-NG architecture is highly developed but territorially anchored. Disaster databases and social protection registries do not automatically interoperate, so displaced households can be reclassified or excluded at re-enumeration.

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure and development displacement are present but less central than disaster and data-system cases in the current chapter. The same absorption constraints would apply where project-affected populations require transfer into national systems.

countries.name: Philippines
countries.role: Marawi, 4Ps/Listahanan, post-Haiyan reconciliation, and OFW/IDP contrast.
countries.summary: The Philippines has advanced disaster, social protection, and overseas-worker institutions. Its core problem is not activation capacity but longitudinal continuity across local government, programme cycles, and recovery phases.

mobility.characterization: Outward labour migration is governed through robust longitudinal institutions, while inward migration and domestic displacement receive far less portable protection. This contrast exposes the architecture of membership and portability.
mobility.pattern: External portability stronger than domestic portability

disaster_idp.characterization: The NDRRMC is among the strongest disaster coordination architectures in the region, but its assumptions are time-bound. Marawi showed that the system could activate but not absorb displacement over years.

public_systems.characterization: 4Ps, Listahanan, PhilHealth, DepEd, and DSWD offer strong institutional capacity, but access is interrupted when displaced households cross municipal boundaries or fall between enumeration cycles.
public_systems.pattern: Partial fiscal absorption with portability gaps

institutional.characterization: Whole-of-government disaster coordination is formalized, yet recovery responsibility fragments across housing, education, health, welfare, and local government mandates. No single institution carries longitudinal IDP responsibility.

fiscal.characterization: The Philippines demonstrates both fiscal integration and its limits. 4Ps is legislated and budgeted, but devolved fiscal architecture creates portability gaps when displaced populations move across municipalities.

data.characterization: Listahanan provides a strong registry backbone and post-Haiyan data-matching precedent, but enumeration cycles and cross-municipal moves can interrupt benefits for displaced households.

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure finance is less central than disaster and conflict displacement in the chapter’s Philippines analysis, though reconstruction and housing after Marawi show how projectized recovery can fragment responsibility.

countries.name: Myanmar
countries.role: Institutional reversal, Kachin/Shan protracted camps, social protection collapse, Rohingya statelessness.
countries.summary: Myanmar is the framework’s outer boundary. Where fiscal predictability, mandate stability, and sovereign continuity collapse, sustainable inclusion cannot be presumed; the mapping identifies what reconstruction would need to restore.

mobility.characterization: Migration and displacement are shaped by state-produced statelessness, conflict, labour emigration, and parallel post-coup governance. The 1982 Citizenship Law remains the core categorical exclusion mechanism for Rohingya.

disaster_idp.characterization: Pre-coup disaster and social welfare systems existed but have degraded severely. Conflict IDP displacement is massive and protracted, while humanitarian delivery substitutes for state systems.

public_systems.characterization: Pre-coup social protection expansion through MCCT, social pensions, and disability allowances collapsed after 2021. Health, education, and registration systems have fragmented into state, resistance, and humanitarian channels.

institutional.characterization: Whole-of-government coordination is structurally impossible under competing authorities and conflict. The mapping therefore reads Myanmar less as an absorption case than as a reconstruction requirements case.

fiscal.characterization: Ordinary fiscal integration has been replaced by humanitarian and parallel financing flows. Displacement-related support is short-cycle, externally financed, and not embedded in predictable public expenditure systems.

data.characterization: IDP data are dispersed across humanitarian registration systems, ethnic governance structures, and fragmented state records. No institutionalized transfer mechanism exists into stable national systems.

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure-related displacement exists, but post-coup conflict and institutional collapse dominate the governance landscape. Development finance entry points are constrained by legitimacy, sanctions, and access.
infra_finance.pattern: Reform entry point constrained by legitimacy crisis

countries.name: Vietnam
countries.role: Hộ khẩu as non-interoperability by sovereign choice; planned relocation and infrastructure displacement.
countries.summary: Vietnam shows high state capacity directed toward territorial management rather than protection portability. Fragmentation is often deliberate: systems are designed to make populations legible to the state, not to track displacement vulnerability across boundaries.

mobility.characterization: Household registration governs access to local services and internal mobility. Labour emigration is managed through bilateral schemes, while refugee governance is limited and politically sensitive cases are treated as state matters.

disaster_idp.characterization: Disaster and climate adaptation capacity is strong, including planned relocation in the Mekong Delta, but relocation is framed as spatial development rather than displacement protection.

public_systems.characterization: Health, education, poverty targeting, and social insurance are administered through territorially anchored systems. Moving across commune or registration boundaries can disrupt access regardless of underlying vulnerability.
public_systems.pattern: Service access tied to registered residence

institutional.characterization: Whole-of-government coherence is strong where it serves state spatial and development objectives. The issue is not weak coordination but the orientation of coordination toward territorial control rather than longitudinal protection.

fiscal.characterization: Fiscal responsibility is tied to local administrative units and state development plans. Resettlement support is often project or programme based rather than embedded as portable long-term entitlement.

data.characterization: The registration system is the central administrative infrastructure. Non-interoperability is not merely technical: it reflects a sovereign choice to prioritize territorial legibility over vulnerability portability.

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure and planned relocation are central. Resettlement compensation exists, but records and support do not consistently integrate with poverty or service systems after households cross administrative boundaries.

countries.name: Cambodia
countries.sovereignty: Territorial state-directed sovereignty with weaker capacity
countries.role: Lower Sesan 2, land concessions, commune-boundary resettlement disruption.
countries.summary: Cambodia illustrates how infrastructure and land-based displacement can be managed through project or commune systems without sustained transfer into public services and social protection.

mobility.characterization: The primary mobility issue is labour outmigration to Thailand, while internal displacement is driven largely by land concessions, infrastructure, and dam construction. Refugee law exists but is weakly implemented.

disaster_idp.characterization: Flood and drought governance exists through NCDM, but internal displacement is not recognized through a dedicated longitudinal framework. Climate and Mekong hydrology pressures increase displacement risk.

public_systems.characterization: IDPoor and Health Equity Funds provide important targeting and health-financing mechanisms, but commune-based administration and three-year update cycles create portability and timing gaps for displaced households.

institutional.characterization: Coordination is often driven by development priorities, party-state control, and project management units rather than a protection-oriented displacement framework.

fiscal.characterization: Displacement-related support is commonly handled through project compensation, donor-supported programmes, or locally administered services rather than portable fiscal responsibility.

data.characterization: IDPoor is a significant data instrument but updates and portability are limited. Resettlement and compensation records are not consistently linked to social assistance or service access over time.

infra_finance.characterization: Infrastructure displacement is central. Lower Sesan 2 shows how compensation and relocation packages can be provided while service access and vulnerability continue beyond the project boundary.

countries.name: Lao PDR
countries.role: Xayaburi, Nam Ou/Nam Theun dam displacement, highland resettlement, safeguard gap.
countries.summary: Lao PDR is the clearest infrastructure-finance case. Project safeguards and resettlement action plans can manage bounded impacts but do not govern downstream, cross-jurisdictional, or long-term livelihood disruption.

mobility.characterization: Mobility governance is dominated by labour emigration to Thailand and state-directed resettlement of highland communities. Inward refugee governance is limited despite formal Convention accession.

disaster_idp.characterization: Disaster governance exists but remains weaker than in larger neighbours. The Attapeu dam collapse exposed the interface between disaster response, infrastructure liability, and displaced-community support.

public_systems.characterization: National social protection infrastructure is limited, especially in rural and resettled areas. Health, education, and welfare access often depend on village location and project-specific support.

institutional.characterization: Whole-of-government coherence is organized around development and infrastructure priorities. Resettlement is handled through project bodies and line ministries rather than a cross-sectoral IDP protection mandate.

fiscal.characterization: Displacement support is usually project financed and bounded by resettlement action plans. Limited ordinary social protection capacity makes fiscal embedding difficult after project closure.

data.characterization: Administrative and social protection data systems are less developed than in Indonesia, Vietnam, or the Philippines. Project records rarely provide a pathway into broader social support.

infra_finance.characterization: Hydropower and corridor infrastructure are central. Xayaburi illustrates the mismatch between safeguard-calibrated project obligations and displacement as a longitudinal, cross-border livelihood condition.

cases.name: Health Insurance Card Scheme (HICS)
cases.function: Strongest positive absorption case. Shows how public-health and cost-recovery rationales can extend coverage without formal protection status.

cases.name: Pink cards / stateless persons
cases.function: Shows how administrative documentation can provide access while restricting mobility and resetting eligibility across provincial boundaries.

cases.name: Rohingya Bangladesh-to-Malaysia
cases.function: UNHCR registration in Bangladesh does not travel to Malaysia; protection needs continue while status and recognition reset.

cases.name: Palu earthquake (2018)
cases.function: Disaster-affected households were reclassified as internal migrants or excluded after emergency and registry cycles shifted.

cases.name: Presidential Regulation 125/2016
cases.function: Positive whole-of-government design assigning cross-ministerial responsibilities, while implementation remains fiscally decentralised.

cases.name: Marawi siege (2017)
cases.function: Shows how strong emergency coordination can fail to produce a single longitudinal mandate for protracted displacement.

cases.name: 4Ps / Listahanan after Haiyan
cases.pattern: Partial fiscal absorption with portability limits
cases.function: Shows the promise and limits of registry-linked social protection when households move across municipal boundaries.

cases.name: Myanmar social protection collapse
cases.function: Outer diagnostic boundary: national systems were not merely weak but reversed, requiring reconstruction before absorption can occur.

cases.name: Vietnam hộ khẩu system
cases.function: Shows that data fragmentation can be a design feature of territorial control rather than a technical deficit.

cases.name: Lower Sesan 2 Dam
cases.function: Compensation and relocation did not ensure portable service access after commune-boundary disruption.

cases.name: Xayaburi Dam
cases.function: Shows why safeguards calibrated to project closure cannot govern downstream and long-term livelihood disruption.
